title: Carbon Credit Trading Scheme & Energy Transition Finance
text:
Carbon Credit Trading Scheme & Energy Transition Finance

Evgeny Gromov/iStock

July 1st, 2026

Download the ReportDownloadIndia’s transition toward a low-carbon economy will require significant investment, not only to decarbonize hard-to-abate industries, but also to support workers, communities, and state economies that remain closely tied to fossil fuels. As India develops its Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS), the scheme presents an important opportunity to mobilize domestic revenue at scale and direct it toward the country’s clean energy transition.Global experience shows that well-designed emissions trading systems can generate substantial public revenues when allowances are auctioned and the proceeds are reinvested in low-carbon technologies, clean energy, energy efficiency, and social and economic support. India could similarly use the CCTS to generate more than USD 500 billion by 2050 through auctioning of allowances for the power sector alone.This opportunity comes at a critical moment. Industrial sectors such as steel, cement, aluminum, and paper will require substantial additional capital to decarbonize, while coal-dependent states face risks related to public revenues, employment, and community welfare. A predictable and transparent finance mechanism can help ensure that CCTS revenues support both industrial transformation and a just transition for fossil fuel–dependent regions.This new report, Carbon Credit Trading Scheme & Energy Transition Finance: Preparing India for Optimal Use of Compliance CCTS Revenue, proposes a CCTS-enabled Energy Transition Finance Mechanism centered on two complementary funds: a Power and Industrial Decarbonization Fund and a State Social and Economic Transition Fund. The report sets out recommendations for governance, fund allocation, monitoring and evaluation, and a phased implementation roadmap for generating and disbursing CCTS revenues to support India’s low-carbon transition.
